title: The disciples argue part 5
scripture_text: ‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire. And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’
devotional:
Mark 9:33-37

‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said, ‘’Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.” He took a little child whom he placed among them. Taking the child in his arms, he said to them, ‘’Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.”

The expectation of Jesus.

In verses Matthew 18:7-9 Jesus explains how we can avoid becoming a stumbling block or a skandalon to others.

1.Realise that we live in a fallen world.

'Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to sin. Such things must come but woe to the man through whom they come.' Jesus was a realist and he understood that we living in a fallen world, a world that is opposed to God. Having said that, Jesus goes on to talk to the disciples about how they must act towards their own sin, especially because they live in a fallen world.

Living in a fallen world does not justify the sinful behaviour of the Christian. If we are the wrong kind of influence on people, causing them to be entrapped in some kind of sinful habit, we cannot blame our circumstances, or the world in which we live. Those who are not free themselves will lead others into sin and bondage. Twice in these verses Jesus uses the phrase 'causes you to sin' this is the same word skandalon. In other words, if you are trapped yourself, it won't belong before you will start trapping others.

2. We must treat our own sin more seriously than anyone else's sin. 'If your hand or foot causes you to sin, cut it off and throw it away and if your eye causes you to sin gouge it out and throw it away.' Jesus is saying, be decisive and if need be, be desperate to cut sinful habits out of your life.

Now there is a problem that we need to address. Maiming yourself will not make you less sinful, because how we deal with our sin is a matter of our hearts. But if we have a right heart attitude towards our sin, then we will go to desperate steps to be free from it. The words that Jesus spoke here was not only extreme hyperbole but they have some historical background

In 2 Maccabees 6-7 (a book in the Apocrypha), the story is told of the martyrdom of the elderly scribe Eleazar and the Jewish mother and her seven sons. Though facing torture and death, Eleazar refuses to renounce his faith, even to pretend to do so, "lest many of the young should suppose that Eleazar in his 90th year has gone over to an alien religion, and through my pretence, for the sake of living a brief moment longer, they should be led astray because of me" (2 Macc 6:24-25; cf. 4 Macc 6:12-23).

In other words, Eleazar refuses to set a poor example and so become a stumbling-block for the Jewish youth of his day. In the vivid expansion of this story found in 4 Maccabees, Eleazar tells his tormentors, "I will not transgress the sacred oaths of my ancestors concerning the keeping of the Law, not even if you gouge out my eyes and burn my entrails" (4 Macc 5:30). So also in the case of the mother and her seven sons. Because they refuse to eat pork, they too face torture and death. The first son has his tongue cut out and his hands and feet cut off (2 Macc7:4). His brother tells the tyrant Antiochus, "Gladly, for the sake of God, we let our bodily members be mutilated" (4 Macc 10:20).

Thus, Jesus' graphic descriptions are not only hyperbolic, they also call to mind the heroes of the faith, who by their devotion to the Law of God set an example for others to follow. They were willing to give up life and limb, rather than lapse into apostasy or lead others into apostasy. As far as Jesus was concerned, his followers could not be any less committed. The fight against our own sin is really a fight for real life. As we deal ruthlessly with our own sin Jesus says ‘we will enter into life.’ As we deal with our own sin, we discover that we enter into the life that God has for us. This does not mean that we become legalistic and self-righteous. Rather we realise that the sin we so often resort to in order to find life, is the biggest threat to real life.

If we are 'entering into life' if we are dealing honestly with our own sin, if we respond with rigour to remove the things that facilitate our sin, then we will be less likely to become a stumbling block to others.

We never intend to become stumbling blocks or a hindrance to others, but we will be by default, if we are not rigorous in confronting our own sin.
prayer: Father teach me to hate my sin more than anybody else's sin.
html:
The disciples argue part 5

Study Text: Matthew 18v1-9. Mark 9v33-37
‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire. And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.’’

Prayer: Father teach me to hate my sin more than anybody else's sin.
Bible in a year reading plan: - 2 Samuel 4-7
